Software project manager still remains as a human-centered strategic role, that can be assisted by agentic AI, rather than a replacement. My recent grey literature review on practitioners' current GenAI experience in SPM pointed me to the vision of "agentic SPM" era, which I plan to build upon and explore the potential to have an "agentic project manager" as an assistant to the human project manager. I aim to validate this vision with practitioners' perceptions, explore PM role evolution and upskilling requirements for the agentic SPM era. I also aim to explore possible SPM education restructuring strategies to include agentic AI into curricula and update evaluations based on industry-demanded skills. While creating the foundation for new research possibilities, my PhD also guides AI developers on developing ethical agentic systems and practitioners on responsible human-agent collaborations.
speaker: Lakshana Assalaarachchi

sessions.title: Next Level: a maturity model in construction for the evolution of software teams based on performance indicators
sessions.abstract: This paper presents the initial stages of a doctoral research project that proposes Next Level, a maturity model under development aimed at supporting the evolution of software teams through performance- based indicators. The model emerged from a practical need iden- tified at Fundação Paulo Feitoza (FPFtech): to understand, in a con- textual and measurable way, how teams evolve in an on-demand software development environment-distinct from digital product companies-while balancing agility, business value, and techni- cal sustainability. The model was collaboratively constructed by People and Process (PP) analysts through workshops that com- bined qualitative reflection and quantitative structure. The resulting indicators were grouped into four dimensions: Business/Efficacy, Process/Efficiency, Culture/People, and Technical/Quality. Data col- lection followed a dynamic inspired by the Spotify Squad Health Check but adapted to the organization's socio-technical context. Unlike prescriptive frameworks such as CMMI and MPS.Br, Next Level does not aim at certification; instead, it provides an evolu- tionary path grounded in measurable outcomes. The model was applied to 14 software teams, producing consolidated dashboards and action plans monitored cyclically. The current research goal is to consolidate this experience theoretically, compare it with exist- ing frameworks, and establish a scientific foundation for its broader generalization, including the use of artificial intelligence.
speaker: Sergio Ricardo de Oliveira Cavalcante

sessions.title: Towards Behavior-Space Testing of Critical ML-Enabled Systems
sessions.abstract: In safety-critical machine learning (ML)-enabled systems, the internal logic of ML component is inherently opaque, and keep evolving by retraining, or architectural modification. While these changes undermine the validity of existing test results, black-box testing alone is insufficient to capture these shifts in internal logic. We propose a novel testing approach that estimates the behavior-space geometry, an abstract geometric space representing the internal behavioral similarity that the ML component forms among data. We seek to estimate behavior-space by combining signals derived from the ML comopnent's learning process, and define test coverage based on the geometry. When the behavior-space is altered by ML evolution, we plan to estimate geometric changes to detect regions of coverage gaps in the existing test suite and select supplementary test suite. Mar 2022 ~: Ph.D. Candidate in School of Computing from KAIST. (Advisor: In-Young Ko) Mar 2020 ~ Feb 2022: M.S. in School of Computing from KAIST. (Advisor: Doo-Hwan Bae) Mar 2015 ~ Feb 2020: B.S. in School of Computing & Business and Technology Management from KAIST.
speaker: Eunho Cho
affiliation: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

sessions.title: Toward Intelligent, Reliable, and Secure Automation of CI/CD Workflows
sessions.abstract: CI/CD pipelines form a core component of contemporary software engineering, automating key stages of the development lifecycle. Since its release in 2019, GitHub Actions (GHA) has rapidly emerged as the dominant CI/CD platform on GitHub, automating millions of workflows daily. However, as the ecosystem grows, developers face persistent challenges in automating GHA workflow creation, mitigating workflow failures, and ensuring security. To address these challenges, my doctoral research systematically examines how developers construct, maintain, and secure GHA workflows in practice, and proposes learning-based approaches to improve their automation quality. The research includes two empirical studies on workflow reliability (reruns and flakiness) and security (evolution of developers' practices), along with two learning-based frameworks, CIGAR for recommending reusable actions and FLOWSTEP for generating step-aware workflows. Together, they aim to build an intelligent and secure automation framework for GitHub Actions integrating reliability, security, and semantic understanding.
speaker: Jiangnan Huang

sessions.title: SoS Governance in Smart Cities: A Tertiary Review Highlighting Research Gaps in the Global South
sessions.abstract: Smart cities have evolved into complex systems‐of‐systems (SoS), integrating physical infrastructures, digital platforms, and social processes. Governing such systems poses critical challenges, particularly in the Global South, where socio‐economic conditions, institutional capacity, infrastructural gaps, and inclusion dynamics differ substantially from the Global North. This research conducts a tertiary review to systematically synthesise existing secondary reviews on smart-city governance, with a focus on identifying research gaps specific to the Global South. The study follows the PRISMA methodology to ensure rigor in article selection and employs Design Science Research (DSR) to guide the creation of conceptual and constructive artifacts. Additionally, the Goal-Question-Metric (GQM) model structures data extraction and analysis. Preliminary findings reveal a strong geographic bias toward high-income regions, limited attention to context-sensitive governance frameworks, weak integration of participatory platforms such as digital twins and DAO/Decidim, and a lack of governance mechanisms tailored to the Global South. Expected contributions include a refined research agenda and a structured methodological pathway to support future studies in SoS governance for smart cities in under-represented regions.
speaker: Gilmar Teixeira Junior

sessions.title: Architecture Conformance Checking for Microservice-based Systems
sessions.abstract: Microservice-based architectures (MSA) offer scalability, flexibility, and modularity, but their distributed and heterogeneous nature increases the risk of architectural deviation and erosion. Ensuring consistency between the planned and implemented architecture, known as Architectural Conformance Checking (ACC), is particularly challenging in MSA due to their distributed nature, heterogeneous technologies, and dynamic communication mechanisms. This research proposes an AI-assisted approach for ACC in MSA, addressing both structural and behavioral dimensions. This work aims to advance the state of the art by integrating AI into the ACC process, improving automation, accuracy, and scalability in architectural conformance verification.
speaker: Ednilson Geraldo Rossi

sessions.title: Shifting the Maintenance Burden: Towards Data-Driven Dependency Management
sessions.abstract: Modern software systems rely heavily on open-source dependencies, yet maintaining these dependencies remains challenging due to the conflicting requirements of freshness, compatibility, and security. Existing tools provide only partial support and often rely on client-specific information, which does not generalize or scale to efficiently manage dependency sets across diverse clients. As a result, developers remain largely responsible for ensuring the quality of their dependency sets. This PhD project investigates how dependency managers can be empowered to provide dependency sets that are up-to-date, compatible, and secure. I hypothesize that this requires rich, fine-grained, and reusable knowledge about how dependencies evolve and interact, which is not currently available or exploited by existing resolvers. To address this, I propose a data-driven dependency management approach that aggregates detailed compatibility and security information and incorporates it into fine-grained, optimizable resolution constraints. The project will contribute techniques for client-agnostic compatibility analysis, a dataset of fine-grained library changes, a hybrid client-aware compatibility and vulnerability detection approach, and an optimization-based dependency resolver tailored to client usage. Together, these contributions aim to reduce the developer burden of dependency maintenance and strengthen the software supply chain.
speaker: Cathrine Paulsen

sessions.title: Large Language Models for Code Refactoring
sessions.abstract: This PhD proposal investigates the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) for code refactoring. We present and discuss three studies. The first evaluated LLMs in identifying and refactoring maintainability issues, showing that, despite their limitations, the results are promising. The second introduced MaRV, a refactoring dataset designed for prompting techniques. The third is an ongoing study in which we will evaluate the performance of several prompting techniques applied to different refactoring types. This proposal also presents our contributions and the future agenda of my PhD.
speaker: Henrique Gomes Nunes

sessions.title: An Empirical Study on Maintenance and Evolution Processes of Embedded Software
sessions.abstract: Embedded software is pervasive in safety-critical dommains such as healthcare, automotive, aerospace, and the Internet of Things (IoT). Its maintenance and evolution are challenging due to hardware constraints, real-time requirements, and long life cycles, often exceeding 10-20 years. Studies indicate that 60-90% of software costs are related to evolution and maintenance. However, empirical evidence shows that current industrial practices remain fragmented, lacking integrative processes and specialized tools. This doctoral research addresses this gap by combining a systematic literature review (SLR), an industry survey, and the design of an integrative process supported by a software tool. The work will be validated in a real-world medical scenario: a low-cost insulin infusion pump. Preliminary results highlight heterogeneity in industrial practices, lack of standardized metrics, and difficulties in handling technical debt and obsolescence. The expected contributions include a unified process, an operational tool, and empirical evidence from both industry and academia.
speaker: Aloysio Carvalho

sessions.title: Challenging Agile: Empirical method's tailoring validation at large-scale
sessions.abstract: Depending on the software system to be developed, software development frameworks can yield different results depending on the complexity of the systems to be developed. While Plan-Drive or Sequential Software Development frameworks, e.g. Waterfall, have demonstrated their value developing simple systems; iterative or agile, e.g. Scrum, has done similarly with complex systems. Agile frameworks has demonstrated their success on small companies or start-ups, however, while scaling them to large-scale enterprises, the results are not as successful as expected. Deciding on the Software Delivery Life Cycle (SDLC) approach is an ambiguous challenge for teams working under the analysis of requirements for software development. Agile frameworks, such as Scrum, Kanban , or eXtreme Programming (XP), and more specifically agile techniques, fit quite well in those uncertain contexts and propose different perspectives in the referred frameworks that face quite a lot of challenges and cause friction when trying to apply them in the referred enterprises. These challenges and frictions can be caused by a variety of variables, such as enterprise culture, team maturity, individuals, or psychological safety, among others. The objective of this Ph. D. is to (1) synthesise existing empirical evidence via a Systematic Mapping Study, (2) use the results of this mapping study to propose a flexible and adaptative evidence-based framework for validating agile techniques in large-scale scenarios, and (3) empirically validate the framework in large-scale enterprises.
speaker: Francisco Gracia Ahufinger

sessions.title: Advancing Language Models for Code-related Tasks
sessions.abstract: Recent advances in language models (LMs) have driven significant progress in various software engineering tasks. However, existing LMs still struggle with complex programming scenarios due to limitations in data quality, model architecture, and reasoning capability. This research systematically addresses these challenges through three complementary directions: (1) improving code data quality with a code difference-guided adversarial augmentation technique (CODA) and a code denoising technique (CodeDenoise); (2) enhancing model architecture via syntax-guided code LMs (LEAM and LEAM++); and (3) advancing model reasoning with a prompting technique ($\mu$FiX) and an agent-based technique (Specine). These techniques aim to promote the practical adoption of LMs in software development and further advance intelligent software engineering.
speaker: Zhao Tian

sessions.title: A Strategy-based Framework for Exploratory Playtesting in Open-World Games
sessions.abstract: Bugs in video games can frustrate players and compromise product success. Ensuring quality in these complex, dynamic systems requires systematic testing practices grounded in software engineering. While automated testing has advanced in other domains, games still depend on manual playtesting to capture the player's experience and detect emergent behaviors that automation cannot easily replicate. This research proposes xPloiT, a strategy-based framework for exploratory playtesting. The framework organizes testing through domain-specific strategies that guide playtesters in planning, executing and documenting test sessions. The resulting structured playtraces will serve as data for modeling agentic playtesting systems that emulate exploratory behaviors. The ultimate goal is, through xPloiT, to create human-like agents capable of reproducing real testing behaviors. This work aims to contribute to both a human-informed and semi-automated testing in complex game environments.
speaker: Yohan Duarte

sessions.title: Toward a Multi-Agent System for Reliable Mocking of Static Methods and Continuous Integration
sessions.abstract: Code written in statically typed languages such as C# often contains static method calls that make methods untestable. Static method calls should be refactored into a custom class and interface to reduce untestable code. This refactoring effort may be unfamiliar to junior developers and tedious to developers with more experience. Traditional unit test generators and, more recently, LLM based unit test generators struggle to automate this refactoring and test creation process. This research aims to investigate the use of an agentic loop (generate, test, repair), multi-agent collaboration, and its incorporation into continuous integration workflows to automate refactoring and test creation. The ultimate goal of this research is to enhance the automated generation of unit tests for production code that includes static method calls, by facilitating accurate test creation and refactoring while minimizing the cognitive load on developers.
speaker: Jasper Stone

sessions.title: Power Relationships Effects on Social Sustainability Goals in Software Ecosystems
sessions.abstract: Software ecosystems (SECO) comprise multiple actors collaborating around a common technological platform, typically governed by a keystone organization. Power relationships define how decisions are made, who holds influence, and how value is shared. When power becomes concentrated, it can undermine social sustainability goals such as fairness, inclusion, and trust. Despite its importance, this topic remains underexplored in software engineering, particularly in applied research. This study investigates how power relationships affect social sustainability in SECO and proposes PReSS-SECO (Power Relationship Effects on Social Sustainability in Software Ecosystems), a framework to diagnose and mitigate power imbalances. This framework includes a conceptual model, a diagnostic tool, and a feedback mechanism for governance improvement. These artifacts will be developed and evaluated through Design Science Research (DSR), based on literature reviews, a participatory case study, focus groups with experts, and validation por evaluation. The research aims to offer information technology (IT) managers a practical means to assess and address power asymmetries, fostering more inclusive and sustainable SECO governance.
speaker: Juliana Outão
affiliation: UNIRIO - Universidade Federal do Estado do Rio de Janeiro

sessions.title: Human-Centered AI for Software Engineering
sessions.abstract: The rise of Artificial Intelligence (AI)-especially Generative AI-has transformed software engineering (SE) practices. Yet, SE remains fundamentally human-centered, requiring attention to behavioral, cognitive, and social factors throughout the development life cycle. Despite this, most industrial and academic efforts towards AI integration emphasize technical aspects, hindering practical success. This Ph.D. project aims to develop a scientific understanding of how AI tools, such as Large Language Models, can be integrated into the SE life cycle without compromising software quality or practitioners' well-being. It investigates the enablers and barriers of AI adoption and their effects on workflow adaptation, particularly within the emerging paradigm of Human-AI teaming. The results will guide practitioners in adopting AI ethically and sustainably, fostering both productivity and well-being.
speaker: Theocharis Tavantzis

sessions.title: An Empirical Perspective on the Lifecycle and Quality Attributes of AI Artifacts
sessions.abstract: Open-source platforms such as Hugging Face have become central infrastructure for Artificial Intelligence (AI), hosting large collections of machine learning (ML) models, large language models (LLMs), datasets, and pipelines. These ecosystems enable rapid reuse and experimentation, but little is known about how AI artifacts evolve over time, which factors drive their quality, and how to engineer them in a reliable, maintainable, and sustainable way. This doctoral thesis adopts an empirical Software Engineering (SE) perspective to: (i) characterize the lifecycle and evolution of AI artifacts in large-scale repositories; (ii) identify and model the determinants of key quality attributes, including maintainability, evolvability, and energy efficiency; and (iii) turn these insights into decision-support frameworks and tools for practitioners in AI-based systems. The thesis combines large-scale repository mining, longitudinal analyses, controlled experiments, and tool-building. Overall, it aims to offer an empirically grounded foundation for engineering, evaluating, and supporting quality-aware AI artifacts across their lifecycle.
speaker: Joel Castaño Fernández

sessions.title: Evolving Artificial Ecosystems: Towards the Infusion of Multi-Agent Systems with Inclusive Inheritance
sessions.abstract: With recent breakthroughs in the field of large language models, the number of "AI agents" deployed into software systems is growing rapidly. As software systems, they must be continuously modernized to avoid system atrophy. In this paper, we present our early-stage research to infuse multi-agent systems with inclusive inheritance, i.e., with evolutionary principles that rely on both genetic and non-genetic inheritance. As part of this doctoral thesis, we aim to conceptualize and implement inclusive inheritance for software systems and evaluate if this holistic approach leverages the potential of bio-inspired evolution of software systems better than relying only on a selection of evolutionary dimensions.
speaker: Alessandro Giugno

sessions.title: Intelligent Approach for Fostering Research Collaborations between Academia and Industry
sessions.abstract: The adoption of new technologies in industry and the public sector is strongly influenced by collaboration among Industry, Academia, and Government. Research and Development initiatives play a central role in this process by driving digital transformation and fostering the creation of applied technologies. This work proposes the development of Systematic Literature Reviews (SLR and TSL), open innovation projects, and an intelligent solution based on Natural Language Processing and Large Language Models to enhance these collaborations. To date, two comprehensive SLRs have been completed, R&D innovation projects have been conducted, and two versions of an MVP have been developed, with the aim of enabling structured information sharing and strengthening the interconnectedness among institutions and researchers.
speaker: Denis Marques
Cleyton Rodrigues
Wylliams Santos

sessions.title: Automating Code-Change Rationale Generation to Support Software Maintenance Tasks
sessions.abstract: Understanding the reasons(rationale) behind code changes is essential for effective software maintenance, yet developers often struggle with fragmented documentation and the absence of clear, actionable rationale explanations. This research aims to bridge that gap by enabling fine-grained, personalized, and task-specific explanations of why code changes occur. We (1) develop techniques to extract and generate multi-document, fine-grained rationale summaries, (2) model developer profiles to tailor explanations to their expertise and familiarity, and (3) generate task-specific rationales for activities such as code review, debugging, and onboarding. Together, this work contributes new empirical insights, a cross-artifact and fine-grained rationale benchmark, and an automated tool for personalized, task-aware rationale generation to advance developer understanding and support software maintenance.
speaker: Mehedi Sun

sessions.title: Multi-Level Testing of Conversational AI Systems
sessions.abstract: Conversational AI systems combine AI-based solutions with the flexibility of conversational interfaces. However, most existing testing solutions do not straightforwardly adapt to the characteristics of conversational interaction or to the behavior of AI components. To address this limitation, this Ph.D. thesis investigates a new family of testing approaches for conversational AI systems, focusing on the validation of their constituent elements at different levels of granularity, from the integration between the language and the AI components, to individual conversational agents, up to multi-agent implementations of conversational AI systems.
speaker: Elena Masserini

sessions.title: A Methodological Framework for Intelligent Vulnerability Management in Microservice Architectures
sessions.abstract: The increased adoption of microservices architectures has changed both how modern applications are designed and operated, while at the same time increasing the attack surface and challenges in cybersecurity. The vulnerabilities in such environments pose one of the most important risks since they directly impact the reliability, integrity, and system availability. Despite their impact, however, much of today's literature relies on synthetic datasets or overly controlled scenarios-a fact that noticeably constrains reproducibility and further diminishes the validity of findings in the face of real-world problems faced by developers. This paper proposes an intelligent methodological framework for the comprehensive management of vulnerabilities in microservices architectures, aimed at overcoming this gap. The research begins with issue mining in GitHub repositories, where genuine reports of flaws and weaknesses in distributed systems are found. Using Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, semantic patterns and emerging topics will be identified and aligned with widely used cybersecurity frameworks such as CVE, CWE, and OWASP. This process will allow for the construction of a hybrid taxonomy, consistent with both real-world practice and formal domain standards. With the resulting dataset, multiple machine learning models will be trained and evaluated, including traditional approaches and deep language models such as DistilBERT. The goal is to develop a classifier capable of automatically anticipating and categorizing new vulnerabilities present in issues from previously unanalyzed repositories, providing predictive capabilities and adaptability to emerging threats. The proposed methodological framework will finally include an assisted mitigation phase, where the vulnerabilities identified will be matched with descriptions, solutions, and remediation practices documented in collaborative databases such as CVE, CWE, NVD, and OWASP. In this respect, the classifier will not only identify the vulnerabilities and classify them but also will suggest corrective actions, technical recommendations, and mitigation measures; thus, it will provide practical and automated support for developers along the secure development lifecycle. The expected outcome is the open dataset with DOIs, the reproducible pipeline for mining, analysis, and classification, as well as the intelligent system that is able to detect, prioritize, and suggest mitigation for vulnerabilities in microservices architectures. This contribution aims to strengthen the practice of secure software engineering and advance the understanding and automated handling of weaknesses in modern distributed systems. Software engineer with 16+ years of industry experience in software development, architecture, and project leadership. Has worked on business-critical systems, including ERP solutions, and has experience spanning IT operations, network administration, and security. Strong track record in analytical problem solving, cross-functional teamwork, and communicating technical content to diverse audiences. Currently focused on research in Machine Learning for Software Engineering.
speaker: Danelys Brito Gonzalez

sessions.title: Interpretable AI with MLIR (IAM):A Unified Framework for Neural Network Transparency
sessions.abstract: Modern AI interpretability tools operate in isolation, each requiring separate forward passes and adding 100-1000ms latency, making real-time AI safety auditing impossible. This research addresses this fragmentation by treating interpretability as a first-class compilation primitive. IAM (Interpretable AI with MLIR) unifies fragmented tools (SAELens, Captum, Petri, LangChain) into a single compiler-integrated framework built on JAX/XLA. By defining custom MLIR dialects for sparse autoencoders, attribution, and policy steering, and leveraging compiler optimization through fusion passes and memory reuse, IAM targets 10-100x performance improvements. The framework introduces novel skip-transcoder architectures for real-time steering while maintaining model accuracy. Initial prototypes demonstrate substantial reduction in memory transfers through compiler fusion. This work aims to enable millisecond-latency safety auditing for production AI systems, bringing mechanistic interpretability from research into deployment.
speaker: SHAKTHI BACHALA

sessions.title: Information Flow-Based Quantification of Timing-Based Software Systems
sessions.abstract: With the growing volume of data stored in software systems, protecting confidential information has become increasingly critical. Consequently, ensuring information secrecy should be addressed throughout the software development lifecycle. While existing approaches support confidentiality analysis at design time and runtime, practical tools for detecting and evaluating side channels, among others timing channels, lack maturity to be easily used by software developers. Because side channels are often not explicitly represented in system artifacts, they go unnoticed even after deployment, pausing serious threat to sensitive information. In my thesis, I intend to present an approach for integrating timing channel analysis into different stages of the software system's development. The goal is to bridge theoretical foundations with practical tools that developers can use to assess and improve system confidentiality. To support flexible decision-making, particularly in case a tradeoff between confidentiality and other properties are necessary, the approach will include quantitative metrics. It will also enable modular analysis, allowing results from individual component to be aggregated and interpreted at the system level. Once developed, I will be evaluate the approach through one or more case studies.
speaker: Fatma Chebbi

sessions.title: Reinforcement Learning from Testing Targets for Effective SLM Test Generation
sessions.abstract: Unit tests are essential for software reliability, yet their manual construction is time-consuming, motivating automated test generation. To support this task, Small Language Models (SLMs) have been examined as a more efficient and privacy-preserving alternative to larger models, though they often fall short in coverage and fault detection. Recent reinforcement-learning approaches address this limitation, but their effectiveness remains constrained by the use of suite-level reward signals that aggregate the contributions of all generated tests. This coarse feedback limits the model's ability to learn targeted testing behaviors, such as exercising specific branches or killing individual mutants. To address these issues, we introduce Reinforcement Learning from Testing Targets (RLTT), a target-driven formulation in which SLMs are optimized using reward signals derived from individual testing goals, inspired by search-based software testing. We will study how different target-level reward designs and reinforcement-learning algorithms shape the acquisition of goal-directed testing behaviors through an empirical evaluation on real-world applications. The expected contributions include a principled framework for optimizing models for target-driven test generation, guidelines for designing reward signals that capture partial or complete goal achievement, and specialized SLMs capable of generating effective tests for specific targets.
speaker: Kevin Céspedes-Arancibia

sessions.title: Rebuilding AI from the Small: The Path Toward Democratized Intelligence
sessions.abstract: AI is reshaping Software Engineering, but the tools with the highest capabilities remain difficult for many researchers and organizations to rely on. Some are accessible only through closed interfaces that change unpredictably, hindering reproducibility and long-term scientific progress. Others require hardware far beyond the reach of typical academic labs or industry teams, limiting who can meaningfully participate in this technological shift. Small Language Models offer a practical middle ground: they are open, efficient, and deployable in controlled environments. Yet they continue to lag behind larger models in tasks requiring deep reasoning. This Ph.D project aims to bridge that gap by studying how the form and structure of prompts influence model performance, and by developing new reasoning frameworks inspired by principles of human cognition. By enhancing the problem-solving abilities of smaller models, this work supports a future in which advanced AI capabilities are accessible, auditable, and usable by the broader SE community.
speaker: Antonio Della Porta

sessions.title: Towards a Software Supply Chain Security Barometer: A Model to Assess and Guide Community Efforts
sessions.abstract: Despite worldwide efforts across communities invested in software supply chain security, vulnerabilities continue to rise. Although more resources can be invested, a sustainable long-term solution would be to develop a model to assess the state of software supply chain security, guiding community efforts and direction. The goal of this dissertation proposal is to aid the software supply chain community in assessing and guiding organizational and ecosystem efforts by developing a barometer model of software supply chain security. We develop a barometer model to systematize the relationships between software security task adoption and security outcomes, providing feedback loops between both. The barometer model guides software organizations in prioritizing the adoption of software security tasks and policymakers in proposing data-driven efforts to improve software supply chain security.
speaker: Sivana Hamer

sessions.title: Towards Systematic Treatment of Community-Driven Variability
sessions.abstract: Modern decentralized software ecosystems such as Bitcoin evolve through crowdsourced improvement proposals (IPs) that are continuously shaped and autonomously implemented by independent actors. As a result, these ecosystems exhibit Community-Driven Variability (CDV), a novel paradigm that extends beyond traditional variability-intensive systems and introduces new challenges that remain unsolved. With this thesis, we approach these challenges and step forward to enable a holistic, systematic treatment of this problem space. This includes (i) establishing a modeling formalism that adequately captures the versatility of CDV ecosystems, (ii) providing tool support for automated model extraction from the large corpus of open-source IP data, and (iii) integrating these mined models with analyses of concrete implementation derivatives. I am a PhD Candidate in the Software Engineering Group (SEG) at the University of Bern. My research centers around secure and robust software, applied cryptography, and software variability in general. Additionally, I am passionate about decentralized privacy-preserving technologies that emphasize user sovereignty.
speaker: Roman Bögli

sessions.title: Detection of Optimization-introduced Bugs in C Compilers
sessions.abstract: Modern C compilers play a critical role in software development, yet they still contain defects that can lead to incorrect binary programs. Optimization passes are especially risky because they apply aggressive code transformations. However, existing testing methods focus mainly on core correctness and give limited attention to optimization behavior. Thus, many optimization-introduced bugs remain hard to detect, and their broader impact is not well understood. My doctoral research will investigate how current compiler testing approaches perform in detecting optimization-related defects. I plan to conduct a large-scale study that examines bugs reported by existing testing frameworks together with those documented in official compiler repositories. This study will help identify recurring patterns in optimization failures and highlight weaknesses in current testing practice. Using the insights from this analysis, I will design an optimization-oriented fuzzing framework that guides test generation toward transformation contexts that frequently lead to incorrect binary program. I will also build an accompanying optimization-focused test suite to support compiler developers and researchers. The overall goal of my work is to provide a deeper understanding of how optimization interacts with testing and to improve the reliability of modern C compilers.
speaker: Abdullah Al Noman

sessions.title: Towards Task-Agnostic Evaluation Framework for LLM-Embedded Applications
sessions.abstract: Evaluating Large Language Model (LLM) embedded applications presents fundamental challenges distinct from traditional software testing due to non-deterministic behaviour across diverse contexts. Existing benchmarks fail to capture domain-specific requirements, while practitioners resort to ad-hoc strategies that are time-consuming and inconsistent. This research proposes a task-agnostic evaluation framework that automatically synthesises evaluations from reusable principles rather than requiring custom configurations. The framework employs a meta-model capturing essential task properties to enable automated evaluation generation across objective and subjective tasks. Through systematic literature review, grey literature analysis, and practitioner validation, we establish guidelines for evaluation selection across diverse LLM task types. Industry case studies validate the framework's practical effectiveness, measuring generalizability, performance, time efficiency, and workflow alignment. This research establishes methodological foundations that shift LLM evaluation from task-specific design to systematic synthesis from reusable principles.
speaker: Dilani Widanapathiranage
affiliation: Applied Artificial Intelligence Initiative, Deakin University

sessions.title: System Comprehension and Source Code Manipulation in eXtended Reality
sessions.abstract: Comprehending a codebase requires developers to construct mental models spanning multiple levels of abstraction, from architectural overviews to individual components. These components differ across paradigms (e.g., classes in object-oriented systems, functions in functional programming). Software visualization supports this process by translating abstract software structures into perceptually meaningful forms that align with human cognitive processes. When grounded in familiar metaphors, such as cities or landscapes, visualization can further narrow the gap between abstract program constructs and developers' mental models. How can language-agnostic expressive metaphors, direct manipulation, and extended-reality be integrated to create immersive, explorable virtual-city environments that help developers explore, understand, and refactor complex, large software systems? Our research advances this idea by extending the city metaphor to represent classes and packages as buildings and districts, where architectural details encode structural and behavioral properties. We classify classes into Class Archetypes using heuristics based on metrics, intra-class, and inter-class relationships. We then develop a specific building for each archetype: Utility classes become factories, data classes become depots, constant definers become archives, and adapter classes appear as surrounding structures. Building on this foundation, we aim to develop a unified, interactive, and immersive visual language for program comprehension.
speaker: Mattia Giannaccari
affiliation: REVEAL @ Software Institute - USI, Lugano, Switzerland

sessions.title: Human-Centric Integration of Large Language Models into Software Engineering Tasks
sessions.abstract: Software practitioners who are used to the constant emergence of new technologies for software development face a pivotal point. Traditional Software Engineering (SE) is currently undergoing a transformation influenced by the arrival of Large Language Models (LLMs) in supporting, or apparently handling, many SE tasks - from requirement engineering to software maintenance and management. However, the existing literature offers a limited understanding of how LLMs affect software practitioners. By employing a human-centric perspective, this PhD research project aims to investigate the multi-faceted nature of how software practitioners are influenced by the revolution in software engineering driven by LLM tools, as well as to suggest improvements to these tools that align with developers' needs. Our research findings will include valuable recommendations and guidelines that can assist software team leaders, IT managers, software engineering educators, and novice practitioners in comprehending the implications of LLM adoption and effectively managing the associated trade-offs.
speaker: Samuel Ferino

sessions.title: Execution-Aware Code Documentation through Semantic Data Differences
sessions.abstract: Automated code documentation has gained increasing importance as AI-assisted software engineering tools become integrated into the software lifecycle. However, most approaches rely solely on static features of code, overlooking its dynamic behavior-particularly in data-centric programming environments where code meaning is deeply tied to its effect on data. This research explores how execution-aware code documentation can be achieved by introducing semantic data differences, a structured symbolic representation of how code execution transforms data. By capturing these runtime semantics, this work aims to extend code understanding beyond syntax and lexical features. I propose a multimodal modeling framework that combines static code analysis with post-execution semantics. In support of this framework, I design a data collection pipeline that executes real-world data-centric notebooks, logs variable-level changes, and abstracts them into a grammar of semantic data differences for training and evaluation. This ongoing doctoral research aims to enable documentation systems that describe not only how code is written, but also what code does to data-a step toward more transparent, reproducible, and intelligent data-centric software.
speaker: Giacomo Fantino

sessions.title: Toward Self-Adaptive and Decentralized Intelligent Software Systems: A Federated Learning Perspective
sessions.abstract: Modern software systems are increasingly deployed in large-scale, heterogeneous, and continuously evolving environments. However, traditional software architectures that rely on static configurations and centralized management are insufficient in these settings, as systems must dynamically adapt to runtime changes, diverse data distributions, and varying user contexts. We aim to advance the foundations of self-adaptive and decentralized intelligent software systems capable of rapid deployment, personalized adaptation, and continuous evolution in large-scale heterogeneous environments. In contrast to conventional self-adaptive systems that focus on runtime configuration, our notion of it extends to intelligence-oriented adaptation, where intelligent software components-such as models and agents-continuously learn, coordinate, and evolve within distributed ecosystems. Building on this, we explore the architectures and principles that enable these systems to self-organize, adapt, and maintain in dynamic environments. This research envisions a new generation of decentralized intelligent software systems that can autonomously maintain scalability, resilience, and privacy while sustaining long-term adaptability and personalization.
speaker: Xiangchi Song
affiliation: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

sessions.title: Comprehensibility-guided Automated Code Refactoring
sessions.abstract: Code comprehension is essential for many software engineering tasks, including code refactoring. Although numerous techniques exist for automated refactoring, none explicitly incorporate code comprehensibility as a guiding principle. The goal of this thesis is to enable comprehensibility-guided automated refactoring. To achieve this, we identify three fundamental challenges in current comprehension research: (1) the lack of rigorous, expert-grounded comprehension proxies; (2) the inherent noise in existing proxy measurements; and (3) limitations in current feature sets and models for capturing the cognitive complexity of human code understanding. This thesis addresses these challenges by establishing validated comprehension proxies, developing more robust relative comprehensibility models, and evaluating modern learning methods-including large language models-for predicting code comprehensibility. Ultimately, this work aims to provide a reliable foundation for automated refactoring techniques that explicitly consider the code understandability as a confounding factor for refactoring code.
speaker: Nadeeshan De Silva

sessions.title: A Multi-Modal Robotic Framework for Physical GUI Interaction Testing
sessions.abstract: Automated testing of mobile applications remains challenging when the goal is to reproduce realistic user interactions and verify GUI behavior without relying on invasive mechanisms such as instrumentation or ADB-level control. Traditional approaches like DLD and R-DLD detect data loss caused by activity restarts, but their dependence on system-level access limits applicability across real devices and heterogeneous environments. This doctoral research proposes \textbf{RFNIT}, a multi-modal, non-invasive robotic framework for physical GUI interaction testing on smartphones. RFNIT enables end-to-end automation that perceives the device state through external sensing and performs physical interactions that closely approximate human behavior while preserving reproducibility and precision. The framework integrates a vision-based perception module, a reasoning agent powered by large language models (LLMs), and a robotic actuator capable of executing taps, swipes, and other gestures. RFNIT collects multimodal signals-external camera feeds, bounding-box detections, and OCR-extracted text-to identify GUI elements and determine subsequent test actions. Preliminary results show that RFNIT can successfully recognize buttons, text fields, and virtual keyboards from external images, achieving over 85% visual detection accuracy. Future work will focus on improving robustness under varying lighting conditions, enhancing repeatability across long testing sessions, and expanding coverage of complex GUI states.
speaker: Davi Simoes Freitas
Breno Miranda
Juliano Iyoda
affiliation: Centro de Informática at Universidade Federal de Pernambuco

sessions.title: Preventing Failures of Smart Human-Centric Ecosystems
sessions.abstract: Smart Human-centric Ecosystems (SHEs), like smart cities, emerge from the co-existence of heterogeneous systems with independently defined specifications. SHEs may fail despite the correct behavior of the systems that comprise the SHE, yet current approaches overlook the critical need of ensuring the reliability of SHEs. Our main goal is to ensure the reliability of SHEs by testing, predicting and ultimately preventing failures in SHEs. We propose (i) a clear and precise definition of the quality of SHEs in terms of healthiness and failures, (ii) SEM, a transformer-autoencoder anomaly detection approach to predict SHE failures, (iii) an approach to prevent SHE failures and restore the normal state of the SHE, (iv) MAD, a multi-agent architecture for Digital Twins (DTs) of SHEs.
speaker: Niccolò Puccinelli

sessions.title: Mapping and Mitigating Bottlenecks in the Linux Kernel Development Model
sessions.abstract: The undeniable success of the Linux kernel project, both as a vital software to society and as a prime example of Free/Libre and Open Source Software (FLOSS), is the result of a sustained, large-scale, and collaborative development effort spanning more than three decades. Nevertheless, its growing complexity, reliance on \textit{ad-hoc} tooling, and dependence on a small set of key maintainers are points of concern that threaten its long-term sustainability. Toward supporting the project's continuity, this PhD research focuses on understanding the processes and practices (workflows) that define the Linux development model by (1) formulating and validating a theoretical framework that characterizes core workflows and associated bottlenecks, and (2) creating free software tools that mitigate identified bottlenecks through automation. Ethnographic case studies are the primary research method to avoid distance from the practice, which is common in academic studies on the topic. However, we employ a multi-method approach that leverages a multivocal literature review to reduce the bias in our findings, as well as concepts of grounded theory to develop our theoretical framework iteratively. Preliminary results include concept maps that describe core workflows, along with two tools, \textit{kworkflow} and \textit{patch-hub}, as well as insights gained from interactions with the Linux community.
speaker: David Tadokoro

sessions.title: Green AI by Default: Energy Reduction Techniques for LLMs in SE
sessions.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being applied to Software Engineering (SE) tasks, showing high accuracy in various problems. However, their high computational demands and energy consumption raise sustainability concerns and hinder their use on consumer hardware and resource-constrained platforms. Multiple optimization techniques exist, but they are often neglected due to the technical difficulty of applying them during model development. This research aims to improve the accessibility of optimization techniques by (1) making energy reporting of LLM more accessible, (2) streamlining and automating optimization techniques, (3) providing guidelines to select appropriate techniques for different use cases, and (4) exploiting these techniques to design efficient SLM ensemble architectures for LLM-enabled applications. Expected contributions include methods for measuring and reporting energy usage, tools to automate compression of models, guidelines to guide developers through the optimization process, and using these results to explore alternative deployment setups for compressed LLMs. PhD Student in Computer Science at the Software Engineering Group at TU Delft. I previously finished my Master's in Software Technologies at TU Delft and my Bachelors in Software Engineering at the University of Seville. My research focuses on Sustainable Software Engineering and Green AI and improving the energy efficiency of AI models. I work with Luís Cruz and the SELF Lab for medical IoT edge devices.
speaker: Enrique Barba Roque

sessions.title: Trustworthy Resolution of Requirement Conflicts at Runtime
sessions.abstract: As software systems become increasingly autonomous, trust has emerged as a critical factor that directly influences users' acceptance and adoption of the system. However, trust is challenged by conflicting system requirements as they can lead to unexpected and potentially unwanted system behaviour. Particularly in autonomous systems that operate in dynamic and uncertain environments, conflicts are likely and need to be managed in a way that preserves and positively impacts the user's trust. This thesis investigates how conflicting requirements can be managed at runtime in a way that maintains user trust. The resulting contributions aim to establish guidelines for trustworthy conflict management that ensure that conflict resolution is both effective and aligned with user expectations.
speaker: Antonia Welzel
affiliation: Chalmers University of Technology and University of Gothenburg

sessions.title: The Dynamics of Knowledge Concentration in Long-lived Collaborative Software Codebases
sessions.abstract: The organizational factors that govern a development team, such as which parts of the code each developer works on or how code changes are reviewed, are pivotal to the survival of large software codebases. Balancing such organizational factors is an even greater challenge for Free/Libre and Open Source Software (FLOSS) projects, which must sustain hierarchies of maintainers while preventing any of them from becoming overloaded with too much responsibility or concentrating too much knowledge of the codebase. This PhD project aims to identify the effects of different development models on the evolution of knowledge concentration in large, long-lived FLOSS projects. To that end, we envision three phases for the project, each with its expected contribution: (A) A new metric to quantify knowledge concentration considering commit authorship and reviewing duties; (B) A tool to visualize the evolution of knowledge concentration in software codebases; and (C) A taxonomy of patterns of development models for mitigating knowledge-concentration-induced bottlenecks in collaborative codebases.
speaker: Arthur Pilone

sessions.title: Testing Programs Expecting Highly Constrained Inputs
sessions.abstract: Current test generators are limited in the inputs they produce. This leads to an imbalance in the program parts tested, with significant program parts receiving insufficient attention, particularly with programs expecting inputs that are highly structured. In this work, I propose two measurements to evaluate different approaches and find which parts of programs are under-tested. Input correctness provides a level to which an input satisfies the expected structure, measured either against an implementation or specification. Input interestingness checks the distance to equivalence class boundaries. I present the distribution of input correctness from an initial evaluation on a clang, indicating that approaches designed to provide structurally valid inputs are unable to test program logic behind the input validation steps. I finally propose three approaches to closing this gap by producing inputs that are both correct and interesting.
speaker: Valentin Huber
affiliation: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security

sessions.title: Toward Intelligent Feedback Systems in Programming Education: AI Tutors, Large Language Models, and Future Directions
sessions.abstract: Large programming courses rely often on automated programming assessment systems (APASs) to orchestrate exercises, tests, and feedback at scale, yet many deployments still provide generic hints, static test suites, and little visibility into the help students receive. My dissertation investigates how large language models (LLMs) can be embedded into APASs so that feedback becomes context aware, trustworthy, and aligned with learning goals rather than only with correctness. Building on my previous work and to round up my thesis, I propose two interrelated work packages. First, I will extend the Iris chat tutor with retrieval augmented generation over course artifacts to improve relevance and reduce hallucinations. Second, I will design a programming education focused LLM arena that compares models on tutoring and exercise generation tasks. These contributions aim to yield reusable tools and empirical evidence for the design of intelligent feedback systems in programming education. At the doctoral consortium, I seek feedback on the research design, evaluation strategies, and scope of these work packages for the final phase of my PhD. I am currently a Doctoral Student in Computer Science at the University of Innsbruck. Specializing in research regarding LLMs and their implications for programming education.
speaker: Eduard Frankford

sessions.title: Studying and Automating Issue Resolution for Software Quality
sessions.abstract: Effective issue resolution is crucial for maintaining software quality. Yet developers frequently encounter challenges such as low-quality issue reports, limited understanding of real-world workflows, and a lack of automated support. This research aims to address these challenges through three complementary directions. First, we enhance issue report quality by proposing techniques that leverage LLM reasoning and application-specific information. Second, we empirically characterize developer workflows in both traditional and AI-augmented systems. Third, we automate cognitively demanding resolution tasks, including buggy UI localization and solution identification, through ML, DL, and LLM-based approaches. Together, our work delivers empirical insights, practical tools, and automated methods to advance AI-driven issue resolution, supporting more maintainable and high-quality software systems. I am a Ph.D. Candidate at Department of Computer Science at William & Mary. I am working as a Research Assistant of Dr. Oscar Chaparro in the Software Evolution and Analysis (SEA) Lab. My research interest includes software engineering, software maintenance and evolution, bug triage and resolution, and deep learning for software engineering. Currently, I am leading four research projects including investigating issue resolution patterns, identifying solution from developers discussion, buggy UI localization, and bug report assessment & improvement using LLMs. Besides this, I am a Lecturer in the Department of Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) at Dhaka University of Engineering & Technology (DUET), Gazipur, Bangladesh. Currently, I am on a study leave from DUET for pursuing my Ph.D. degree. Prior to this I worked as a Lecturer in the Department of Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) at Military Institute of Science and Technology (MIST), Bangladesh. I graduated with a B.Sc. degree in Computer Science & Engineering (CSE) from Rajshahi University of Engineering & Technology (RUET), Rajshahi, Bangladesh.
speaker: Antu Saha

sessions.title: Data-driven Test Generation for Fuzzing AI Compiler
sessions.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) compilers are critical for efficiently deploying AI models across diverse hardware platforms. However, they remain prone to bugs that can compromise both compiler reliability and model correctness. This work presents a unified data-driven testing framework that systematically addresses stage-specific challenges in AI compilers. OPERA migrates AI library tests to cover the model loading stage. OATest synthesizes diverse optimization-aware computational graphs for testing high-level optimizations. HARMONY generates and mutates diverse low-level IR seeds to generate hardware-optimization-aware tests for testing low-level optimizations. Together, these techniques provide a comprehensive, stage-aware framework that enhances testing coverage and effectiveness, detecting 266 previously unknown bugs in four widely used AI compilers.
speaker: Qingchao Shen

sessions.title: Towards Cognitive-Agent-Driven Prompt Optimization for Automated Program Repair
sessions.abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have significantly reshaped software engineering workflows, particularly in Automated Program Repair (APR). Despite their potential, LLMbased repair techniques often exhibit unstable behavior, limited generalization and strong prompt sensitivity. This proposal investigates a research direction that unifies Reinforcement Learning (RL), cognitive agents and self-adaptive architectures to autonomously optimize prompts for LLM-based patch generation. We hypothesize that treating prompt design as an adaptive optimization problem can significantly improve stability and correctness. By embedding prompt refinement in a continuous feedback loop, and enabling agents to maintain persistent beliefs extracted from repositories and execution traces, the repair process becomes more stable, explainable and effective.
speaker: Felipe Gaia
Marcelo De Almeida Maia
affiliation: Federal Institute of Education, Science and Technology Goiano (IF Goiano)

sessions.title: Plan4code: Planning Code Changes with Chain-of-Thought and Ontologies
sessions.abstract: This doctoral research investigates how software engineering on- tologies combined with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting can make LLM-based code changes more systematic and explainable. I propose Plan4code, a novel approach that leverages Large Lan- guage Models (LLMs) to plan, execute, and explain code changes. The research comprises (i) a literature review on Artificial Intelli- gence (AI) techniques for legacy software; (ii) the design and im- plementation of the Plan4Code architecture; and (iii) an empirical evaluation to assess its effectiveness in automating and improving code changes
speaker: Andrey Justo
Jefferson Seide Molléri
Luiz Eduardo G. Martins

sessions.title: An Incident Management Approach Based on Site Reliability Engineering Practices to Support Governance in Proprietary Software Ecosystems
sessions.abstract: This study investigates how Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) practices can enhance incident management as a governance mechanism within Proprietary Software Ecosystems (PSECO). These ecosystems operate under contractual, regulatory, and intellectual property constraints that complicate collaboration and reliability assurance. To address this challenge, we adopted a structured empirical design grounded in Empirical Software Engineering principles, combining a systematic mapping study, a multivocal literature review, and a participative case study with a global organization. The results led to the design the Supported Integration Approach (SIA) aimed at integrating human expertise, process, and technological enablers. Early findings indicate that the approach improves incident traceability and knowledge transfer among teams while aligning reliability practices with governance objectives. This research contributes theoretical, methodological, and practical insights for embedding SRE practices into PSECO governance, promoting a sustainable environment.
speaker: Luiz Alexandre Costa
affiliation: UNIRIO - Universidade Federal do Estado do Rio de Janeiro

sessions.title: Utilizing LLMs for Industrial Process Automation
sessions.abstract: A growing number of publications address the best practices to use Large Language Models (LLMs) for software engineering in recent years. However, most of this work focuses on widely-used general purpose programming languages like Python due to their widespread usage training data. The utility of LLMs for software within the industrial process automation domain, with highly-specialized languages that are typically only used in proprietary contexts, remains underexplored. This research aims to utilize and integrate LLMs in the industrial development process, solving real-life programming tasks (e.g., generating a movement routine for a robotic arm) and accelerating the development cycles of manufacturing systems.
speaker: Salim Fares
affiliation: University of Passau, Faculty of Computer Science and Mathematics, Chair of AI Engineering

sessions.title: Tailoring Software Engineering Generative AI Agents to Company Standards and Developers' Preferences
sessions.abstract: One of the hurdles of integrating generative AI (GenAI) tools into existing software engineering workflows is that the outputs generated by the foundational models are not tailored to specific company standards and developers' individual preferences. This challenge has prevented many companies from adopting AI-assisted software engineering across their workflows. This PhD project aims therefore at investigating in the next three years different methods and tools for providing tailored and personalized GenAI outputs taking both company standards and developers' preferences into account. The objective is to facilitate GenAI integration into existing software engineering workflows and boost adoption across different software engineering activities.
speaker: Pedro Fiuza Linhares
affiliation: The Maersk Mc-Kinney Moller Institute, University of Southern Denmark

sessions.title: SE4AI: Structured AI/ML Enabled Software Development Process
sessions.abstract: Most organizations developing software that depends on AI/ML models often rely on ad hoc or unstructured development practices. In many cases, the model and the surrounding software are developed separately, leading to several problems-particularly regarding the quality of the model, the compatibility of the system in which the model is embedded, and the overall suitability of the resulting system to the problem domain. Although prior research has addressed specific aspects of this process and proposed improvements for isolated phases, a comprehensive, quality-oriented development framework is still lacking. This doctoral research aims to propose an enhanced development process that addresses these gaps and opportunities, ensuring both methodological rigor and the delivery of reliable, high-quality AI/ML-enabled systems.
speaker: Felipe Sonntag Manzoni

sessions.title: ContextPilot: Code Context Engineering with Memory-Augmented Exploration Agents
sessions.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have been widely adopted to address real-world repository-level software engineering tasks. However, such tasks pose significant challenges for LLMs due to the need to reason over vast contexts spanning multiple files and modules. Code context engineering, the process of identifying relevant code context, plays a pivotal role, yet existing approaches face substantial limitations. Retrieval-based methods rely on static strategies that often fail to capture enough information. Agent-based methods, while effective, typically depend on large models for end-to-end task solving, incurring considerable computational costs. Moreover, the extensive interactions in agent-based methods lead to lengthy input sequences, which not only increase computational overhead but also impair model's ability to leverage prior information. To address the limitations, we propose ContextPilot, a novel framework that effectively and efficiently discovers code context for complex repository-level software engineering tasks. ContextPilot comprises three key components. First, it employs a decoupled explorer-generator agent structure where a lightweight explorer model efficiently navigating repositories and a large proficiency model then generates the final answer using the collected context. Second, a tool-based memory mechanism periodically converts exploration history into concise textual summaries, ensuring the model's input context remains within a manageable length. Third, we introduce a reinforcement learning-based training method augmented with cold start distillation to optimize the explorer's capabilities in context exploration and memory management. We conduct preliminary experiments on repository-level code generation and question answering benchmarks, DevEval and LongCodeBench. The results demonstrate that ContextPilot achieves competitive or even superior performance compared to large model-based agent methods while substantially reducing computational costs.
speaker: Shuzheng Gao
Chaozheng Wang
Shuqing Li
Yun Peng
Michael Lyu
affiliation: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
